summary_paragraph:
We will not
set aside the HAB’s order “except for errors of law,” unless we are satisfied, “by
a clear preponderance of the evidence” before us, “that such order is unjust or
unreasonable.” RSA 541:13 (2021). The HAB’s factual findings “shall be
deemed to be prima facie lawful and reasonable.” Id.
The petitioners argue that because the requested use of their property is
permitted under the Ordinance, the HAB erred in affirming the ZBA’s decision.
The HAB refused to consider this argument because it concluded that the
petitioners never made this argument before the ZBA. Instead, the HAB
decline

summary_paragraph:
The Town argues that despite its issuance of the CRO, a preexisting
nonconforming use cannot be established “[i]f the use of the [property] was in
violation of the applicable zoning ordinances at the time.” The Town asserts
that the property was in violation of zoning ordinances in two ways: first, there
was an ADU on the property (which triggers additional zoning ordinance
requirements of owner occupancy and minimum rental periods); and second,
the CRO was invalid because the plaintiffs’ CRO application did not disclose
the ADU. See HZO §§ 3-A.4(a) (requiring owner occupancy of either the ADU

summary_paragraph:
-15 (setting
forth standard to determine whether due process requires a pre-deprivation
hearing).
As the appealing party, the plaintiff has the burden of demonstrating
reversible error. Gallo v. Traina, 166 N.H. 737, 740 (2014). Based upon our
review of the trial court’s well-reasoned order, the plaintiff’s challenges to it, the
relevant law, and the record submitted on appeal, we conclude that the plaintiff
has not demonstrated reversible error. See id.
Affirmed.
MacDonald, C.J., and Donovan, Countway, Gould, and Will, JJ.,
concurred.
Timothy A. Gudas,
Clerk

summary_paragraph:
The defendant received actual notice of the hearing and appeared at the
hearing in her defense. To that end, she testified at the hearing that, “I appear
today because I am the Respondent.” As to whether the protective order
properly identifies the defendant, the trial court granted, in part, the
defendant’s post-hearing motion, ruling that the final order shall be amended
to properly identify the defendant by name and physical description.
Accordingly, we find no error.

summary_paragraph:
RSA 273-D:3, V provides, in relevant part: “In all cases, the [PAB] may
reinstate an employee or otherwise change or modify any order of the
appointing authority, or make such order as it may deem just.” DAS contends
that the portion of this statutory language permitting the PAB to issue any
order it deems “just” provides an insufficiently defined standard to guide the
PAB, thereby violating the separation of powers article of the New Hampshire
Constitution. See Appeal of Blizzard, 163 N.H. 326, 331-32 (2012).
Part I, Article 37 of the State Constitution sets forth the separation of
powers for

summary_paragraph:
We assume the petitioner’s
pleadings to be true and construe all reasonable inferences in the light most
favorable to the petitioner. Id. However, we need not assume the truth of
statements in the petitioner’s pleadings that are merely conclusions of law. Id.
at 56-57. We then engage in a threshold inquiry that tests the facts in the
petition against the applicable law. See id. at 57. We will uphold the trial
court’s grant of a motion to dismiss if the facts pleaded do not constitute a
basis for legal relief. Id.
Resolving the petitioner’s appeal requires that we engage in statutory

summary_paragraph:
However, the rule should be used sparingly, its use limited to those
circumstances in which a miscarriage of justice would otherwise result. Id. To
find plain error: (1) there must be an error; (2) the error must be plain; (3) the
error must affect substantial rights; and (4) the error must seriously affect the
fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings. Id.
When instructing the jury, the trial court stated multiple times that the
jury was required to decide the case solely on the evidence presented at trial.

summary_paragraph:
We have previously explained that the term “detain” means “to hold or
keep in or as if in custody,” “to keep back,” and to “stop or delay.” State v.
Lindsey, 158 N.H. 703, 706 (2009) (quotations omitted). We have also explained
that the phrase “seeking to effect an arrest or detention” includes “the entire
course of events during which law enforcement officers seek to secure and
maintain physical control of an individual, attendant to accomplishing the
intended law enforcement duty.” Id. (quotation omitted). “Whether a defendant’s
resistive conduct occurred while law enforcement was ‘seeking t

summary_paragraph:
Some evidence means more than a
minutia or scintilla of evidence. Id. To be more than a scintilla, evidence
cannot be vague, conjectural, or the mere suspicion of a fact, but must be of
such quality as to induce conviction. Id. However, the evidentiary support
need not be overwhelming, nor must the evidence presented be uncontradicted.
Id. When there is simply no evidentiary basis to support the theory of the
requested jury instruction, the party is not entitled to such an instruction, and
the trial court may properly deny the party’s request. Id. Because we are
asked to review the “trial cour

summary_paragraph:
We assume the truth of the relevant well-pleaded factual
allegations in the plaintiff’s pleadings and construe all reasonable inferences
from those facts in the light most favorable to the plaintiff. See id. We do not,
however, assume the truth of statements in the plaintiff’s pleadings that are
conclusions of law. See id.; ERG, Inc. v. Barnes, 137 N.H. 186, 190 (1993)
(“The court will not, however, assume the truth or accuracy of any allegations
which are not well-pleaded, including the statement of conclusions of fact and
principles of law.”). We will uphold the trial court’s decision if the
